coredata.dc:title: Spectrally-Selective and High-Resolution Visualization Detection of UVR Based on Efficient Photo-Oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Simin
Lin, Yao
Ren, Tian
Wang, Xi
Zhang, Jinyi
Zheng, Chengbin
coredata.dc:description: Ultraviolet radiation (UVR) is like a double-edged sword, which has dual significance to human life and health. Current UV sensors struggle to accurately discriminate and quantify UVA, B, and C simultaneously. Additionally, common colorimetric sensors have low visualization resolution. Herein, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent UVR sensing strategy with spectrally-selective, accuracy, significant color change, and adjustable sensitivity based on the photo-induced fluorescence quenching properties of carbon dots (CDs) and the photosensitization of protoporphyrin IX (PPIX). For daily UVR monitoring, a portable fluorescence reader is developed, which can simultaneously detect dose and intensity. Unlike conventional radiometers, it can sensitively and rapidly discriminate and quantify UVA, B, and C in complex light sources at a lower cost (< 100 $). To ensure the safety of UVR use, wearable fluorescent film sensors (< 0.5 $/piece) we developed for real-time visual monitoring of the cumulative dose of UVA/B during UV phototherapy. Considering individuals with color vision deficiency, we propose a strategy to transform “color analysis” into “count analysis” through creative design, making quantification easier. CDs-PPIX composite offers great potential for UVR detection in our daily lives due to its excellent performance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent UVR sensing platform based on efficient photo-oxidation of CDs-PPIX composite for accurate and naked-eye detection of UVR irradiance and cumulative dose.

(a) Mechanism diagram of fluorescence quenching of CDs under UVR irradiation. (b) Chemical structures, ϕ▵, ϕF, and λmax, em of fluorescein derivatives [35,36,41–43]. (c) Effect of UVA (λmax = 365 nm), UVB (λmax = 313 nm), and UVC (λmax = 254 nm) (10 W/m2) exposure times on the fluorescence intensity (450 nm) of CDs (16 mg/L) solutions in the presence and absence of PPIX. (d) Effect of UVA (λmax = 365 nm), UVB (λmax = 313 nm), and UVC (λmax = 254 nm) (10 W/m2) exposure times on the fluorescence intensity (634 nm) of PPIX solutions in the presence and absence of CDs (16 mg/L). (e) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (16 mg/L), CDs + Ar + light (λmax = 365 nm, 18 W/m2), CDs + light (λmax = 365 nm, 18 W/m2), and CDs + PPIX + light (λmax = 365 nm, 18 W/m2). (f) The phosphorescence emission spectra of 1O2 induced by CDs (16 mg/L), PPIX (10 μM), and CDs + PPIX in a CD3CN-D2O mixed solvent (v/v = 15:1) under UVA (λmax: 365 nm, selected with a monochromator, slit: 14.7 nm) (Ex: 365 nm). (g) The phosphorescence emission spectra of 1O2 induced by CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (10 μM) in a CD3CN-D2O mixed solvent (v/v = 15:1) under different UVR (λmax: 365, 300, and 254 nm, selected with a monochromator, slit: 14.7 nm). (h) Schematic diagram of the sensing mechanism of CDs-PPIX ratiometric fluorescent sensing composite for UVR (UVA/B/C).

(a) Keeping the ratio of CDs/PPIX constant (I450/I633 = 5:1), the effect of concentration on CDs/PPIX ratiometric fluorescent sensor: the fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs/PPIX at different concentrations (CDs: 1.6, 3.2, 16, 32, 160 mg/L, PPIX: 0.3, 0.6, 3, 6, 30 μM) before and after exposure to UVA. (b) The ratio of fluorescence intensity (I633/I450) and (c) the fluorescence photos of CDs-PPIX solutions with different initial fluorescence emission intensity ratios (I450/I633) after absorbing different dose of UVA (λmax = 365 nm). Schematic diagram of the fluorescence change principle of CDs-PPIX ratiometric fluorescent composite for (d) UVA, (e) UVB, and (f) UVC sensing. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs-PPIX ratiometric fluorescent solution after being exposed to different dose of (g) UVA, (h) UVB, (i) UVC, the inset is the corresponding fluorescence photos of the solution. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs-PPIX ratiometric fluorescent solution after being exposed to different irradiance of (j) UVA, (k) UVB, (l) UVC for 1 min, the inset is the corresponding fluorescence photos of the solution.

(a) Details of the fluorescence reading device based on the smartphone are shown in the enlarged view. (b) Lighting sample panel with CDs-PPIX composite. (c) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence reading device installed on an iPhone smartphone. (d) Schematic diagram of the internal optical path of the device. (e) Representative mobile phone fluorescence image of the CDs-PPIX solution. (f) Photographs of the device. (g) Photos and flow chart of the testing process. UVR radiation intensity in a day (60 min interval) was detected with (h) ratiometric fluorescent analyzer, (i) ultraviolet radiation meter.

(a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication process of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film. (b) Appearance size, fluorescence, and UV response of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film. (c) Waterproofing properties of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film. (d) Bending deformability of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film. Fluorescent color change of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film with different content of PPIX with increasing dose of (e) UVA and f UVB light.

(a) Diagram of a UV sensor patch used to monitor UV phototherapy. (b) Schematic structure of the CDs-PPIX UV-sensing patch. Schematic of the fluorescence changes in the CDs-PPIX UV-sensing patch under different doses of (c) UVA and (e) UVB irradiation. Photographs of fluorescence changes of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing patches under different doses of (d) UVA and (f) UVB irradiation. (g) Photographs of fluorescence response to UVA of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing patches worn on the skin in the dark. (h) Photographs of fluorescence visualization of CD-PPIX UV-sensing patches applied to the skin in visible light and underwater (under 365 nm UV light). Photographs of the (i) large and (j) small size CDs-PPIX UV-sensing patches worn on the hand under different doses of UVA/B.

head.ce:title: Spectrally-Selective and High-Resolution Visualization Detection of UVR Based on Efficient Photo-Oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultraviolet radiation (UVR) is like a double-edged sword, which has dual significance to human life and health. Current UV sensors struggle to accurately discriminate and quantify UVA, B, and C simultaneously. Additionally, common colorimetric sensors have low visualization resolution. Herein, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent UVR sensing strategy with spectrally-selective, accuracy, significant color change, and adjustable sensitivity based on the photo-induced fluorescence quenching properties of carbon dots (CDs) and the photosensitization of protoporphyrin IX (PPIX). For daily UVR monitoring, a portable fluorescence reader is developed, which can simultaneously detect dose and intensity. Unlike conventional radiometers, it can sensitively and rapidly discriminate and quantify UVA, B, and C in complex light sources at a lower cost (< 100 $). To ensure the safety of UVR use, wearable fluorescent film sensors (< 0.5 $/piece) we developed for real-time visual monitoring of the cumulative dose of UVA/B during UV phototherapy. Considering individuals with color vision deficiency, we propose a strategy to transform “color analysis” into “count analysis” through creative design, making quantification easier. CDs-PPIX composite offers great potential for UVR detection in our daily lives due to its excellent performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet radiation (UVR) was discovered by John Ritter in 1801 as a significant component of solar radiation and can be produced by both natural and artificial sources.[1] The biological effects of UVR (100–400 nm) also vary greatly depending on the wavelength, so the UVR spectrum is further divided into three regions: UVA (315–400 nm), UVB (280–315 nm), and UVC (100–280 nm).[2,3] As UVR passes through the atmosphere, all of the UVC and approximately 90% of the UVB radiation are absorbed by ozone, water vapor, oxygen, and carbon dioxide.[4,5] UVR is utilized across various fields such as medical treatment, lithography, curing processes, communication technologies, and analytical methods, exerting a notable influence on human health and well-being.[6–8] The appropriate level of UVB exposure is crucial for overall health and mental wellness as it promotes the synthesis of vitamin D within the body.[9] UVC demonstrates significant ability to eradicate pathogenic bacteria and viruses.[10] Specifically, UVA and UVB have shown notable effectiveness in treating chronic skin conditions such as psoriasis and vitiligo.[11,12] On the other hand, prolonged exposure to low-energy UVA radiation can lead to premature skin aging,[13,14] whereas intense UVR exposure may result in eye-related ailments.[15,16] Moreover, exposure to UVB radiation poses a significant concern due to its ability to induce substantial DNA damage, making it a primary contributor to the development of skin cancer.[17,18] Overall, various wavelengths of UVR exhibit distinct biological impacts. Consequently, in order to effectively manage the advantages and disadvantages associated with UVR exposure, it is crucial to implement spectrally selective monitoring techniques to track both the immediate intensity and cumulative dose of UVR.

The detection of UVR is influenced by numerous factors, and the sensor should be adaptable and portable. At present, the portable detectors used to detect UVR are mainly divided into two categories: photoelectric sensors and photochromic sensors, each with its own advantages and disadvantages. Photoelectric UVR sensors are characterized by high sensitivity, good accuracy, and fast response. However, these devices are often unable to discriminate between UV types simultaneously, and they requires extensive high cost spectral calibration.[19] Consequently, they are difficult to use in everyday consumer applications.[20,21] Photochromic materials offer the unique ability to detect UVR through a simple color change.[22,23] Unlike the photoelectric UVR sensors mentioned above, which require additional accessories to display changes in the current signal, photochromic sensors can be easily and intuitively read by the naked eye, reducing the inconvenience and cost of use.[24,25] In addition, detecting the degree of discoloration of the photochromic material can effectively reflect the cumulative dose of the material under UVR.[26,27] However, if the color signal intensity is too low, the visual sensitivity of the photochromic sensor is compromised. In addition, human vision is not sensitive enough to changes in the lightness of a single color. Photo-responsive luminescent materials offer similar advantages to the aforementioned photochromic materials. In recent years, the rapid development of portable fluorescence analysis technology and the popularity of smartphones has expanded the application of fluorescent materials.[28,29] Moreover, the combination of photo-responsive luminescent materials with solid composite substrate materials such as paper-based,[26] polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA),[30] and cellulose[31] improves the economy, convenience, portability, and wearability of fluorescent UVR sensors.

As a green fluorescent material with excellent fluorescent properties, carbon dots (CDs) possess unique photo-oxidizing properties.[32] Their fluorescence is affected by UVR due to their distinctive photocatalytic oxidation properties,[33] which holds promise for constructing UVR sensors. However, the catalytic oxidizing ability of the CDs themselves is relatively weak, leading to a low sensing sensitivity for UVR.[32,34,35] Protoporphyrin (PPIX), a red fluorescent dye, can produce more reactive oxygen species (ROS) when exposed to UVR irradiation.[36,37] The combination of PPIX with CDs is expected to enhance the sensitivity of CDs in response to UVR.

In this work, we propose a strategy to construct fluorescent UVR sensors using PPIX-sensitized CDs, where PPIX plays a role in the CDs-PPIX mixture system (Fig. 1 ): 1. As a modifier to regulate the sensitivity of the sensing materials. PPIX generates a large amount of ROS under UVA/B illumination, which enhances the fluorescence quenching of the CDs caused by oxidation. 2. As an ideal reference fluorescent material for constructing a ratiometric fluorescent UVA/B sensor, spanning from blue-violet to red fluorescence. 3. As an indicator fluorescent substance, the CDs acts as a reference fluorescent signal to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for UVC from blue-violet to blue fluorescence. To enhance the portability, accuracy, and sensitivity for the instant detection of UVA/B/C, we have created an affordable (< 100 $) UVR sensor by integrating 3D printing and smartphone technology (Fig. 1). This sensor can discriminate between UVA/B/C in sunlight, detecting the intensity (Limit of detection (LODUVA) = 0.67, LODUVB = 0.69, and LODUVC = 0.072 W/m2) of irradiation over a one-minute average, and measuring the cumulative dose (LODUVA = 16.21, LODUVB = 7.25, and LODUVC = 27.86 J/m2). The sensor successfully determined UVA/B/C irradiance from sunlight. In addition, we have easily combined this composite with PMMA to fabricate low-cost (< 0.5 $/piece), wearable fluorescent film sensors[38,39] for real-time visual monitoring of the cumulative dose of UVA/B during UV phototherapy (Fig. 1). The sensor exhibits excellent durability, specificity, and stability, showcasing great potential for applications in complementary disease treatment. The number of reddened petals in the “flower” patch field allows for visual monitoring of the typical UVA therapeutic dose (2000–30000 J/m2) and UVB therapeutic dose (200–1000 J/m2). In short, due to its adjustable sensitivity and numerous other advantages, the CDs-PPIX composite is applicable in various settings.

To ensure that the test is precise, accurate, reproducible, and safe, all dose and intensity quantification in the experiment was performed in a unified setup, as shown in Fig. S29. Throughout the study, “intensity” indicates the irradiance of the surface of the sample, expressed as W/m2; “dose” indicates the cumulative dose of specific radiation on the sample surface over a period expressed as J/m2 (1 W/m2 = 1 J/m2/s). The UV light sources in the device include different range of wavelengths (UVA (λmax = 365 nm), UVB (λmax = 313 nm), and UVC (λmax = 254 nm)), whose irradiation intensity is calibrated with a standardized radiometer (Fig. S30). In the experiment, when the distance between the sample and the light source is constant, the irradiation duration is changed to obtain different cumulative doses; when the irradiation duration is constant, the distance between the sample and the light source is changed to obtain different irradiation intensities.

RB, PPIX, RhB, Rh6G, PB, and FL-Na (5 μM) were mixed with CDs (4 mg/L) respectively, and the initial fluorescence spectra of six samples were tested (Ex = 365 nm, Em: 380–700 nm). Then the above six samples were exposed to the same intensity of UVA (25 W/m2) for 10 min, and their fluorescence spectra after irradiation were recorded. The fluorescence color of the solution before and after irradiation was recorded with a digital camera respectively.

To explain the effect of PPIX on CDs, the fluorescence spectra of CDs (16 mg/L) without lighting, CDs (16 mg/L) light-treated (λmax = 365 nm, 18 W/m2) for 1 min, CDs (16 mg/L)- PPIX (8 μM) treated with light (λmax = 365 nm, 18 W/m2) for 1 min were tested. To verify the effect of dissolved oxygen, Ar was introduced to remove the dissolved oxygen in CDs (16 mg/L), and its fluorescence spectra was tested. The phosphorescence emission spectra of 1O2 induced by CDs (16 mg/L), PPIX (10 μM) and CDs-PPIX mixture in a CD3CN-D2O mixed solvent (v/v = 15:1) were tested to verify the generation of 1O2 under UVA (λmax = 365, selected with a monochromator, slit: 14.7 nm) irradiation. In addition, to explain the different fluorescence change trends of CDs-PPIX mixtrue under different UVR, 10 W/m2 UVA (λmax = 365 nm), UVB (λmax = 313 nm), and UVC (λmax = 254 nm) light were used to illuminate CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (20 μM) 0–180 s respectively, and their UV spectra were recorded.

For better results, the solvent of the CDs-PPIX mixture was optimized. CDs (6 mg/L) and PPIX (2.5 μM) were mixed in DMSO and ethanol respectively, and both were exposed to UVA (18 W/m2, 5 min)/ UVB (7.5 W/m2, 2 min)/ UVC (18 W/m2, 2 min). The fluorescence of samples in DMSO and ethanol before and after irradiation was detected (Ex = 365 nm). The CDs-PPIX mixing ratio for sensing UVA/B/C was optimized for the strongest color change and the appropriate sensitivities for subsequent experiments.

With DMSO as the solvent, the concentration of CDs (16 mg/L) remained constant while the ratio was adjusted by changing the concentration of PPIX. CDs and PPIX were mixed in the initial fluorescence emission intensity (I450/I633) of 2.5:1, 5:1, and 8:1, and the three ratios of CDs-PPIX mixture (200 μL) were exposed to 18 W/m2 UVA for 0–6 min. The variation of fluorescence intensity ratios and fluorescence color were recorded to obtain the optimal CDs-PPIX mixing ratio for UVA detection. CDs and PPIX were mixed in the initial fluorescence emission intensity (I450/I633) of 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1 in intensity, and the three ratios of CDs-PPIX mixture (200 μL) were exposed to 6 W/m2 UVB for 0–6 min. The variation of fluorescence intensity ratios and fluorescence color were recorded to obtain the optimal CDs-PPIX mixing ratio for UVB detection. CDs and PPIX were mixed in the initial fluorescence emission intensity (I450/I633) of 2:1, 1:1.2, and 1:2.5 in intensity, and the three ratios of CDs-PPIX mixture (200 μL) were exposed to 18 W/m2 UVC for 0–6 min. The variation of fluorescence intensity ratios and fluorescence color were recorded to obtain the optimal CDs-PPIX mixing ratio for UVC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis performance based on fluorescence spectrometer
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (6 μM) was exposed to 18 W/m2 UVA for 0–6 min, and the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color were recorded to investigate the sensitivity of UVA dose detection; 200 μL CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (6 μM) was exposed to 0–35 W /m2 UVA for 1 min, and the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color were recorded to investigate the sensitivity of UVA irradiance detection. 200 μL CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (8 μM) were exposed to 6 W/m2 UVB for 0–6 min, and the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color were recorded to investigate the sensitivity of UVB dose detection; 200 μL CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (8 μM) was exposed to 0–25 W/m2 UVB for 1 min, and the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color were recorded to investigate the sensitivity of UVB irradiance detection. 200 μL CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (8 μM) was exposed to 18 W/m2 UVC for 0–6 min, and the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color were recorded to investigate the sensitivity of UVC dose detection; 200 μL CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (8 μM) was exposed to 0–35 W/m2 UVC for 1 min, and the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color were recorded to investigate the sensitivity of UVC irradiance detection.

To investigate the effect of temperature on the material, the CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (7.5 μM) were heated in a 40 °C water bath for 0–60 s, and the change in the ratio of their fluorescence intensities (I633/I450) was detected. To investigate the effect of infrared, the CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (7.5 μM) were exposed to infrared at 808 nm for 0–60 s, and the change in the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I633/I450) was recorded. Visible light may also affect the fluorescence of the material, irradiate the CDs (16 mg/L)-PPIX (7.5 μM) material for 60 s with 0, 254, 303, 365, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700, 750, 800 nm light (from a xenon lamp, monochromator selected, slit: 29.4 nm), photograph and record it.

The smartphone-based fluorescence reader mainly consists of a 3D-printed device housing, a 3D-printed sample plate, and an optical element embedded in the housing. The 3D printed material used is a black nylon material with a rough and non-reflective surface, and a 75 mm wide slot is designed on the top of the 3D printed housing to attach the device to the phone. The top center opening is convenient for the phone's camera to take fluorescent photos. The lens, plastic diffuser, and LED light source are fixed in the two corner slots on the top. The battery is mounted in the reserved battery slots on both sides of the housing. The sample tray contains three sample slots with a built-in hydrophobic material to hold the fluorescent solution. When used for sunlight detection, bandpass filters of different wavelengths are placed over the sample wells for spectral selection. When used for fluorescence observation and detection, the sample plate and housing are mounted together to form an inspection unit.

The outdoor detection time is 9:00–18:00 on May 10, 2022, at Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan Province, China. The temperature on that day was 18–27 °C, cloudy to sunny, and the relative humidity was 75 %. The operation process is shown in the attached Video 2, including adding CDs-PPIX (200 μL) solution to the three channels of UVA/B/C of the sample plate, covering it with a filter, exposing it to sunlight for 60 s, and taking pictures with a mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing PMMA film
ce:section.ce:para: PMMA (2 g) was dissolved in dichloromethane (15 mL) at room temperature and shaken for 5–10 min for rapid and complete dissolution. Then, 375 μL of CDs (0.8 g/L) in DMSO solution and 150 μL of PPIX (1 mM) in DMSO solution were added to the above PMMA solution, and the PMMA solution containing 20 mg/L CDs and 10 μM PPIX was obtained after complete dissolution. The solution was then poured into each of the prepared molds in a volume of 0.5 mL and dried at ambient temperature (above 25 °C) for 2.5 h, protected from light. When the solvent was completely removed, white translucent circular films with a diameter of 1.5 cm were obtained, and films with smaller dimensions could be obtained by further cutting. Similarly, films with different ratios of CDs and PPIX concentrations could be obtained by the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, durability, and stability of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-PPIX UV-sensing films were subjected to various conditions including placement in a 0 °C refrigerator and an 80 °C oven for 0–600 s, exposure to normal indoor visible light intensity (0.2 W/m2) and high-intensity visible light (20 W/m2) for 0–600 s, immersion in water for 0–1200 s, etc. Subsequently, their fluorescence photos were taken before and after irradiation with 5000 J/m2 UVA, and the R/B values were extracted. We tested the stability of the CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film over 31 days. The same batch of prepared CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film was stored in black opaque plastic self-sealing bags at 4 °C and then its fluorescence was recorded by photography after 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 10, 15, 31 days respectively. Its fluorescence was also recorded after exposure to 5000 J/m2 UVA. The R/B values of the photographs were extracted and evaluated for the stability of the films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of UVA/B dose by CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film at different concentrations and ratios
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain suitable CDs and PPIX concentrations, a batch of films containing the same CDs-PPIX concentration ratio but with different CDs (8–32 mg/L) and different PPIX (2.5–10 μM) concentrations was fabricated. The films with these different concentrations were exposed to a series of standard doses of UVA (0, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 15000 J/m2), and the fluorescent color changes of the films were observed and documented by taking pictures with a camera. Similarly, to obtain CDs-PPIX UV-sensing films with different UV-corresponding sensitivities, we fabricated a batch of films containing the same concentration of CDs (20 mg/L) but different concentrations of PPIX (2.5–27.3 μM). These films were exposed to a series of standard doses of UVA (0, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 9000, 15000, 21000, 30000 J/m2) and UVB (0, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 450, 600, 1000 J/m2) for observation and camera pictures were taken to record the fluorescence color changes of the films. These photographs were cropped using Photoshop and then pooled together for comparison and analysis.

To produce a patch that indicates the exposure dose of 2000, 5000, 9000, 15000, 21000, and 30000 J/m2 UVA, six films containing CDs at concentrations of 20 mg/L and PPIX at concentrations of 3.0, 3.4, 3.8, 5.0, 6.3 and 8.8 μM were arranged in the shape of a “five-petaled flower”. The concentration of PPIX was highest in the center of the “flower”, followed by the uppermost petal, and then the concentration of PPIX in the remaining petals was gradually decreased in a clockwise direction. To minimize visualization errors caused by different wearing backgrounds, a non-fluorescent double-sided adhesive was used to tightly connect the fluorescent film to the black skin adhesive. In the same way, patches signaling the dose of UVB exposure could be obtained after adjusting the concentration of PPIX. The prepared patches were stored at 4 °C away from light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing patch
ce:section.ce:para: To perform UV dosimetry experiments under simulated UVA/B phototherapy conditions, the sensing patch was worn on the arm and exposed to UVA/B light sources with a consistent UVA/B intensity of 25 W/m2 for different exposure times. The fluorescence changes of the patches were observed and photographed at UVA exposure doses of 0, 5000, and 30000 J/m2 and UVB exposure doses of 0, 250, and 1000 J/m2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and mechanism of CDs-PPIX composite for UVR monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: To construct a UVR ratiometric fluorescent sensor with excellent performance, we synthesized CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (QY)[40] by a hydrothermal method, which can be excited in UV and emit in the blue region of the visible spectrum (Ex = 350 nm, Em = 450 nm, Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 2 a and in previous studies,[32,33] this CDs can be oxidized by singlet oxygen (1O2) produced by UVR illumination, leading to its fluorescence quenching. However, this fluorescence quenching behavior is too slow and slight to support sensitive sensing of UVR. Therefore, we screened six common fluorescein derivatives (their structure and optical properties are shown in Fig. 2b) to enhance the sensitivity and accuracy of UVR detection by developing ratiometric fluorescent probes and boosting the generation of 1O2 concurrently. The fluorescence spectra were obtained before and after irradiation, following the same experiment performed after mixing them with CDs at the same concentration (Fig. S2). Combining the data and experimental results, we compared the various properties (1O2 QY (ϕ▵), fluorescence QY (ϕF), maximum emission wavelength (λmax, em))[35,36,41–43] of the six dyes and the degree of fluorescence quenching of CDs after mixing with them. Ultimately, we selected PPIX for its superior overall performance (Fig. S2, 3, and Video 1). To better characterize the response of CDs and PPIX to UVR, their reactions to various types of UVR were examined. CDs responded to UVA, B, and C but the response sensitivities of them were slightly different, as shown by the sequence UVA < UVB < UVC (Fig. S4a). In contrast, PPIX exhibited varying responses to UVA, B, and C (Fig. S4b). It was almost unresponsive to UVA, slightly responsive to UVB, and highly responsive to UVC. Therefore, when we exposed the CDs-PPIX composite to different UVR, they exhibited various fluorescence changes (Fig. 2c, d), giving them spectral discrimination capability. When the CDs-PPIX composite was exposed to UVA, PPIX significantly enhanced the sensitivity of the UVA response of the CDs. The fluorescence of the CDs was significantly decreased, but the red fluorescence of PPIX remained relatively unchanged. The overall fluorescence shifted from blue-violet to red. PPIX enhanced the UVB response sensitivity of CDs, but to a lesser enhancement than when exposed to UVA. Additionally, the fluorescence of PPIX was slightly quenched by UVB, resulting in an overall fluorescence change from blue-violet to violet. When exposed to UVC, the presence of PPIX has no effect on the sensitivity of CDs to UVC; however, the fluorescence of PPIX is significantly quenched by UVC. This resulted in a change in the overall fluorescence from blue-violet to blue. In general, compared to the response of individual CDs (Fig. 2c) or PPIX (Fig. 2d) to UVR, the CDs-PPIX composite exhibited higher sensitivity or a more significant color change in reaction to UVR.

To verify the potential sensing mechanism, the UVR quenching effect of CDs with and without PPIX was compared (Fig. 2e). In the presence of PPIX, the fluorescence quenching of CDs was more significant, indicating a considerable sensitizing effect of PPIX. The degree of fluorescence quenching of CDs induced by UVR after deoxygenation was significantly lower than that without deoxygenation (Fig. 2e), indicating the crucial role of dissolved oxygen. UV-induced oxidation of dissolved oxygen is usually associated with ROS production, as the further confirmed by phosphorescence emission spectra of 1O2 induced by CDs and PPIX. As shown in Fig. 2f, UVA-irradiated CDs, PPIX, and CDs-PPIX composite can all produce ROS, but CDs produce comparatively low levels. The amount of 1O2 produced by CDs-PPIX composite increases with the UVR wavelength (Fig. 2g), resulting in the oxidation of CDs to larger particle sizes[33] and varying degrees of fluorescence quenching of CDs under different UVR conditions. Then, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, PPIX, and CDs-PPIX composite were recorded (Fig. S5), indicating that CDs and PPIX formed a simple mixture without interacting at the molecular level. The UV–Vis spectral variations of CDs-PPIX composite under UVA, B, and C irradiation are presented in Fig. S6. The absorbance of CDs gradually decreased under UVA/B/C with the increase of irradiation time, indicating that the molecular structure of CDs was changed under UV irradiation. The effect of UVA/UVB irradiation on the maximum absorption peak of PPIX is not evident. However, the absorbance of PPIX decreased with UV irradiation. This suggests that the molecular structure of PPIX was destroyed by UVC, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the impact of UVA on absorption spectrum of CDs is greater than that of PPIX (Fig. S6a), and UVB does the same (Fig. S6b); however, the influence of UVC on PPIX is slightly greater than that of CDs (Fig. S6c). This can explain the different response trends of CDs/PPIX materials to UVA/B/C.

Therefore, we can summarize the UVR sensing mechanism of the CDs-PPIX ratiometric fluorescent sensing composite in Fig. 1h. For UVA and UVB, the sensing is mainly attributed to the fluorescence quenching of the PPIX-sensitized CDs. Due to the different ability of PPIX to generate 1O2 under UVA and UVB irradiation, the CDs-PPIX composite exhibited different degrees of fluorescence changes (ranging from blue-violet to red) under UVA and UVB. For UVC, the sensing mechanism is mainly based on the fluorescence quenching of PPIX due to the significant molecular damage caused by UVC. Thus, the fluorescence of CDs-PPIX composite typically shifts from purple to blue in an inverse direction. Here, the response of CDs-PPIX composite to UVR is an irreversible process. Reversible materials, although reusable at a lower cost, can usually only be used for UV instantaneous intensity measurements and cannot record cumulative doses. Therefore, the irreversible property gives the composite the ability to record the UVR cumulative exposure dose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-PPIX ratiometric fluorescent composite for intensity and dose sensing of UVR with adjustable sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent sensitive response to UVR, CDs-PPIX composite can be utilized for quantifying UVA/B/C intensity and dose. It is well known that the solvent affects the physical and chemical properties of the solute, so we optimized the solvent for the CDs-PPIX composite. Using DMSO and ethanol as solvents, after the same dose of UVA/B/C irradiation, it was found that the change in the intensity ratio of the CDs-PPIX composite was significantly greater in DMSO than in ethanol (shown in Fig. S7). Additionally, the UVR sensitivity of CDs-PPIX composite is affected by their concentration, as shown in Fig. 3 a. At the same initial mixing ratio (the ratio of the initial fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs/PPIX mixing solution, I450/I633), an increase in concentration results in a significant enhancement of the response sensitivity to UVR. Similarly, the sensitivity of UVR sensing in CDs can be enhanced by PPIX. Modulation of their sensitivity can be achieved by varying the initial mixing ratio of CDs-PPIX composite, as shown in Fig. 3b, c, and Fig. S8, 9. Increasing the proportion of PPIX results in greater sensitivity of CDs-PPIX composite to UVA/B sensing and reduced sensitivity to UVC sensing. Therefore, the ratios (the ratio of the initial fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs/PPIX mixing solution, I450/I633) of 2.5:1, 2:1, and 2:1 were selected to measure the dose and intensity of UVA/B/C in sunlight, and the most significant color changes and the appropriate sensitivities were obtained for subsequent experiments respectively.

Under optimal conditions, the sensitivity of CDs-PPIX composite to the dose and irradiance of different UVR were quantified. As shown in Fig. 3d, e, f, the CDs-PPIX composite exhibit different response phenomena to UVA, B, and C. The responses of the solution to UVA (Fig. 3g) and UVB (Fig. 3h) exhibit similar trends, with the response to UVA being more sensitive than that to UVB. As the cumulative dose increased, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm decreased, while the fluorescence intensity at 633 nm remained relatively stable. The solution fluorescence gradually transitioned from violet-blue to red. The linear range of response to UVB included the minimal erythematous dose (MED)[44] for six skin tones. However, the response of the CDs-PPIX composite to UVC showed an opposite trend (Fig. 3i). As the cumulative dose increased, the fluorescence intensity at 633 nm decreased, while that at 450 nm remained relatively stable. The fluorescence of the solution gradually shifted from violet to blue. As shown in Fig. 3j, k, l, the fluorescence spectra and fluorescence images of the solution were recorded after 1 min of exposing under different UV irradiance. With the increase in UVA and UVB irradiance, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-PPIX composite at 450 nm decreased, while the fluorescence intensity at 633 nm remained relatively stable. The fluorescence color of solution appears from violet-blue to red (Fig. 3j, k). Conversely, the response to UVC irradiance also showed an opposite trend (Fig. 3l), and the fluorescence color of solution shifted from blue-violet to blue.

To assess the practical application potential of CDs-PPIX composite in the field, we studied the anti-interference performance of the material. In practical applications, solar radiation includes ultraviolet, visible, and infrared. Exposure of the CDs-PPIX solution to high-intensity infrared light and heat at 40 °C for 0–60 s showed a smaller change in the fluorescence ratio, in sharp contrast to the solution exposed to UVB (Fig. S10). Part of the visible light affects the fluorescence of the CDs-PPIX solution, and the visible light around 450–500 nm will slightly influence the fluorescence of the solution (Fig. S11, 12). Therefore, completely avoiding visible light interference and discriminating UVR in complex light source requires the help of filters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based fluorescence reader enables on-site analysis of solar UV dose and irradiance
ce:section.ce:para: To make the application of CDs-PPIX composite for detecting sunlight UVR more portable and accurate, a portable fluorescence reading device was designed as a smartphone accessory, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The device is small and lightweight (size shown in Fig. S13) and can be attached to most types of smartphones to capture fluorescent pictures. The built-in light source and the battery allow for fluorescent color analysis to be conducted anytime and anywhere. The diffuser placed under the light source can help to diffuse the light and reduce background light interference when taking pictures (the excitation light source < 0.05 W/m2). The lens ensures that the excitation light falls on the CDs-PPIX solution as much as possible (Fig. 4a). The lighting sample plate (Fig. 4b) below the device contains three channels with built-in hydrophobic materials. The CDs-PPIX composite solution can be added to it, combined with various types of filters (Fig. S14), enabling the discrimination and detection of UVA, B, and C in sunlight simultaneously. The overall effect and optical pathway diagram after assembly are shown in Fig. 4c, d. Using the device to capture images of various fluorescent solutions (Fig. 4e), the obtained photos exhibit high color saturation, high contrast, and minimal background light interference. To avoid ROS interference generated during the detection process, the time of each test should be short and fixed (< 15 s), and the interference with the results can be eliminated by testing and subtracting the blank solution as background. Furthermore, the device is cost-effective, priced at less than $100 in total.

In the optimal state, the analytical performance of the UVR dose and irradiance sensor based on a smartphone portable fluorometer was investigated. As shown in Fig. S15a, b with the increase in UVA and UVB irradiation dose, the fluorescence of the CDs-PPIX solution gradually shifted from blue-violet to pink-purple. Conversely, the fluorescence of the solution exposed to UVC changed in the opposite direction (Fig. S15c). After extracting the RGB values from the captured images, subsequent calculations unveiled a strong linear relationship between the values of (R + G)/B and the UVR dose within a specific range. The calculated LODs of UVA, UVB, and UVC dose were 16.21, 7.25, and 27.86 J/m2. Similarly, as the intensity of UVA and UVB exposed increased, the color of the solution also changed from blue-violet to pink-purple (Fig. S15d, e), and the solution exposed to UVC changed from pink-violet to blue-violet (Fig. S15f). The RGB values of the fluorescent colors above were extracted, and the LODs for detecting UVA, UVB, and UVC intensity were calculated to be 0.67, 0.69, and 0.072 W/m2.

To investigate the practicality of UVR dose and irradiance sensor, the solar UVR intensity at different times of one day was tested outdoors. The test procedure is shown in Fig. 4g and Video 2 attachment. The lighting sample panel containing the CDs-PPIX solution is exposed to sunlight for 1 min (May 10, 2022, Chengdu), and the average light intensity within 1 min is evaluated by measuring the RGB value. The results obtained by our ratiometric fluorescence analyzer are shown in Fig. 4h. At the same time, the UVR intensity at the same time was also detected by a radiometer (Fig. 4i). The changing trend of UVR intensity obtained by the two methods is consistent, indicating that most of them have no significant difference (Table S4). In detail, all UVR intensities first increased and then decreased. The UVR intensity weakened significantly in the afternoon as the weather transitioned from clear to cloudy. In the detection results of each period, the UVA intensity was the highest, while the UVC and UVB intensities were relatively low. Therefore, based on the detection results, the CDs-PPIX ratiometric fluorescent analyzer demonstrates good practicality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent films for naked-eye UV detection
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the practical application potential of CDs-PPIX composite, we incorporated it into PMMA to create fluorescent film sensors with outstanding performance.[30,45] The CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film is prepared by simple dissolution, mixing, and drying (Fig. 5 a). To ensure the production of fluorescent films that are homogeneous, flat, flexible, and UV-responsive, we optimized various preparation conditions for the films. These conditions included the selection of molds for drying the films, the concentration of PMMA, the organic solvents used for dissolving PMMA, the dispersing solvents used for CDs-PPIX, and the volume of films formed in the molds. These optimizations are detailed in Fig. S16–20. Notably, the addition of DMSO during the preparation process is very important as it helps to enhance the UV sensitivity and flexibility of the films (Fig. S19, Videos 3, 4). This is due to the relative non-volatility and water absorption of DMSO, which makes the films more flexible as the water content increases and produces more reactive oxygen species upon UV irradiation. Finally, the optimal preparation conditions are summarized in Table S5.

Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent films we produced are shown in Fig. 5b. It is white, translucent, approximately 0.2 mm thick, around 15 mm in diameter, and can emit bright and uniform fluorescence. To further assess the practical utility of the UV-sensing film, we conducted a series of studies to evaluate its durability, specificity, and stability. As environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, and visible light tend to vary widely, we first assessed the effects of these factors to determine if they led to a non-specific sensor response. The fluorescence of the films was unchanged under indoor visible light, high temperature at 80 °C, and low temperature at 0 °C. Additionally, there was no effect on the fluorescence response properties to UVA (Fig. S21). Notably, the films must be stored away from light carefully, as strong visible light causes a slight effect (Fig. S22, S23). Furthermore, immersing the fluorescent film in water for 20 min did not significantly alter its fluorescence or affect its UVA response performance, demonstrating its excellent waterproofing (Fig. 5c, Fig. S24). Therefore, these sensors are very robust even when exposed to different temperatures and humidity during UVR sensing. Then, the films were deformed to different degrees in Fig. 5d. Impressively, neither bending nor folding caused any damage to the surface structure and fluorescence of the films. These results show that the prepared films with stable fluorescence behavior can fit well to the skin even under different deformation morphologies, which is crucial for practical applications (Fig. S25). Furthermore, these fluorescent films remained stable for at least 4 weeks (Fig. S26). In summary, these results demonstrate the high specificity, durability, stability, and reliability of UV sensing film made from these CDs-PPIX under different application conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-sensing patch used for monitoring doses during UV phototherapy
ce:section.ce:para: UVA and UVB are commonly used in the treatment of dermatologic conditions.[46,47] However, accurately quantifying a patient’s cumulative UVA/B exposure dose during treatment is challenging with current monitoring methods. This can contribute to more negative effects of treatment. To address this issue, wearable UV sensing patches have been developed to assist with UVA/B phototherapy. These patches can detect UV exposure dose during treatment and help mitigate the negative effects of treatment. Therefore, to design sensing patches that respond to specific therapeutic dose, we then investigated the UVA response characteristics of CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film with different concentrations of CDs and PPIX at a constant CDs/PPIX ratio to obtain optimal concentration conditions. The fluorescent films were initially blue-violet fluorescent, and the fluorescence of the films gradually changed to purple-red with increasing UVA exposure time (Fig. S27). Whereas, with increasing concentrations of CDs and PPIX, the fluorescence color of the CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film changes to purple-red when exposed to lower UVA doses, and the sensitivity of the response to UVA increases. We then selected a constant CDs concentration of 20 mg/L and varied the concentration of PPIX to investigate the effect of different CDs/PPIX concentration ratios on UVA/B response properties. Consistent with our assumptions, the sensitivity of the CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film increased as the PPIX content increased, and the UVA/B dose required to change the fluorescent color to purple-red gradually decreased (Fig. 5e, f). Based on this result, we obtained a specific CDs-PPIX UV-sensing film by pre-experimental screening (the concentrations of CDs and PPIX used to prepare the films are shown in Fig. 5e, f), whose fluorescent changed to exactly purple-red at exposing doses of 2000, 5000, 9000, 15000, 21000, 30000 J/m2 UVA (doses commonly used in Psoralen-UVA (PUVA) phototherapy treatment) and 200, 250, 300, 450, 600, 1000 J/m2 UVB (doses commonly used in Narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) phototherapy treatment) respectively.[11,46] Thus, films of the above specifications can be used to construct sensing patches that respond to specific doses of UVA/UVB.

Considering individuals with color vision deficiency, we propose a strategy to transform its “color analysis” into “count analysis” through creative design, making quantification easier. To achieve this goal, we combined CDs-PPIX films with bonding material to form a “flower” patch for monitoring six commonly used UVA/B doses in UV therapy (Fig. 6 a). The patch consists of three layers: (bottom) a black PVC adhesive layer for skin adhesion (acting as a standardized background), (middle) a PET adhesive layer that connects all the films, and (top) a UV sensing layer (Fig. 6b). The UVA sensing layer consists of six films containing six different contents of PPIX. As the irradiation dose increased, their fluorescence color transitioned to purple-red in sequence starting from film No. 1 in the center of the “flower” to film No. 6 (Fig. 6c), reaching saturation at 2000, 5000, 9000, 15000, 21000, and 30000 J/m2, respectively (Fig. 6d). Similar to the construction and composition of the UVA sensing patch, the UVB sensing layer was constructed using six films containing six different contents of PPIX, and their concentration were higher. As the irradiation dose increased, their fluorescent colors transitioned to purple-red and reached saturation at 200, 250, 300, 450, 600, and 1000 J/m2, respectively (Fig. 6e, f). Therefore, we can easily determine the exact UVA/B irradiation dose received by looking at the number of films that have turned purple-red, thus avoiding the personal visual errors associated with the ordinary fluorescence colorimetry.

In addition, the wearability of wearable sensors and the scenarios in which they can be used are very important for their practical applicability. We have experimentally found that the CDs-PPIX UV-sensing patch has good wearability and UV-sensing capability on both skin and fabric (Fig. S28). What’s more, considering the potential impact of various wearer scenarios on fluorescence color visualization and detection, we evaluated the fluorescence visualization effect of the patches in different wearer scenarios. Good visualization performance in darker conditions as shown in Fig. 6g. As shown in Fig. 6h, brighter visible light conditions do not affect our observation of the patch’s fluorescent color, or even when immersed in water. In summary, the patch has excellent wearability, low cost (< 0.5 $/piece) and a wide range of application scenarios.

As shown in Fig. 6i, j, to better evaluate the practical applicability of this UV-sensing patch, we designed two sizes of patches for different areas of the skin surface. Two sizes of patches were worn on the skin and exposed to UVA/B of fixed intensity. The UV radiometer was used to monitor the UVA/B dose during this time. The fluorescence color of the two sizes of patches changed consistently. The fluorescence color of the “flower heart” of the UVA “flower” patch becomes purple-red firstly. The fluorescence color of the “flower heart” and one of the “flower petals” of the UVA “flower” patch turned purple-red exactly when the cumulative dose of UVA irradiation reached 5000 J/m2. Then when the cumulative dose reaches 30000 J/m2 the fluorescence of the whole UVA “flower” patch all becomes purple-red. When the cumulative dose of UVB irradiation is 250 J/m2, the fluorescence color of the “flower heart” and one of the “flower petals” of the UVB “flower” patch turned purple-red exactly. When the cumulative dose reaches 1000 J/m2, the fluorescent color of the whole UVB patch turns purple-red. Consequently, the UVA/B “flower” patch can accurately detect the UVA/B irradiation dose to the skin.

In this work, ratiometric fluorescent UVR sensing composite with accuracy, significant color change, and adjustable sensitivity are proposed based on the photo-induced fluorescence quenching properties of CDs and the photosensitization of PPIX. PPIX plays a role in the CDs-PPIX mixture system: 1. Increases the sensitivity of the carbon dots to UV light sensing. 2. The construction of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with CDs. 3. The fluorescence of PPIX is quenched during UVC irradiation and serves as an indicator fluorescent substance. As shown in Table S6, in comparison with representative UV sensors, the strategy proposed in this work has certain advantages in terms of fabrication, specificity, selectivity, sensitivity, real-time monitoring potential, and practical applicability. Combining this composite with the portable fluorescence analysis method, a smartphone-based fluorescence reading device with built-in UVA, B, and C channels is designed, which can sensitively and rapidly discriminate and quantify UVA, B, and C in complex light sources. By changing the test mode, the method can detect radiation intensity and dose. In addition, we have easily combined this composite with PMMA to fabricate low-cost, portable fluorescent thin-film sensors for real-time visual monitoring of the cumulative dose of UVA/B received by patients during UV phototherapy. The sensor has excellent durability, specificity, and stability, demonstrating excellent potential for disease treatment applications. In general, the excellent performance of this composite is expected to support the detection of UVR in life, medicine, environment, and agriculture, such as the monitoring of environmental pollutants through the monitoring of UV, and the strict control of light elements for plant growth.
